topic.title: Bayes' Rule
topic.question: How does Bayes' rule update beliefs given evidence, and why is the posterior proportional to likelihood times prior?

lesson.title: Bayes' Rule: Updating Beliefs with Evidence
lesson.payoff: After this lesson you can quantify exactly how a positive medical test result changes a 1% disease prior into a concrete posterior probability — no guessing required
staircase: Define prior probability as belief before seeing evidence
Define likelihood as how probable the evidence is given a hypothesis
Define marginal probability as total probability of seeing the evidence at all
Derive Bayes' rule from the definition of conditional probability
Identify that posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior
Apply the formula numerically to a disease-test scenario
Interpret the result: intuition about base rates dominating rare events
lesson.destination: Learner holds the formula P(H|E) = P(E|H)*P(H) / P(E) and understands each term's role, plus the proportionality shorthand P(H|E) proportional to P(E|H)*P(H)

narration.fullText: Suppose you want to know whether a patient has a disease, before running any test. Your starting belief — called the prior probability — is written P of H, where H stands for the hypothesis that the patient is sick. Say P of H equals 0.01, meaning a one-percent chance based on how common the disease is in the population. Now you run a test and it comes back positive. That positive result is your evidence E. The question Bayes' rule answers is: what is P of H given E — read as the probability of the hypothesis being true after seeing the evidence — which we call the posterior probability. To get there we need one more ingredient: the likelihood, written P of E given H, which asks how probable is it that the test comes back positive if the patient really does have the disease. Suppose the test is 90 percent accurate, so P of E given H equals 0.9. But we also need to account for false positives — cases where the test is positive but the patient is healthy. Call the probability of being healthy P of H-complement, which equals 0.99. And suppose the false-positive rate is 8 percent, so P of E given H-complement equals 0.08. The denominator of Bayes' rule is P of E, the total probability of getting a positive test regardless of disease status — called the marginal probability of E. We compute it by summing over both possibilities: P of E equals P of E given H times P of H, plus P of E given H-complement times P of H-complement. Plugging in: 0.9 times 0.01 plus 0.08 times 0.99, which gives 0.009 plus 0.0792, equaling 0.0882. Now Bayes' rule says P of H given E equals P of E given H times P of H, divided by P of E. That is 0.009 divided by 0.0882, which is approximately 0.102 — about ten percent. Even after a positive test, the posterior is only ten percent because the disease is rare. The key insight is that the numerator is just likelihood times prior, so we say the posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior. The denominator P of E is simply a normalizing constant that makes probabilities sum to one. Write it with the proportionality symbol: P of H given E is proportional to P of E given H times P of H. That proportionality is the engine of Bayesian reasoning.

beats.text: Suppose you want to know whether a patient has a disease before running any test. Your starting belief — called the prior probability — is written P of H, where H stands for the hypothesis that the patient is sick.
beats.whyThisBeat: Establish the prior probability concept and notation as rung 1

beats.text: Say P of H equals 0.01, meaning a one-percent chance based on how common the disease is in the population.
beats.whyThisBeat: Give the prior a concrete numerical value to anchor the example

beats.text: Now you run a test and it comes back positive. That positive result is your evidence E. The question Bayes' rule answers is: what is P of H given E — the probability of the hypothesis being true after seeing the evidence — which we call the posterior probability.
beats.whyThisBeat: Introduce evidence E and the posterior P(H|E) as the goal, rung 1 completed

beats.text: To get there we need the likelihood, written P of E given H, which asks how probable is it that the test comes back positive if the patient really does have the disease. Suppose the test is 90 percent accurate, so P of E given H equals 0.9.
beats.whyThisBeat: Define likelihood and supply its numerical value, rung 2

beats.text: But we also need to account for false positives. Call the probability of being healthy P of H-complement, which equals 0.99. And suppose the false-positive rate is 8 percent, so P of E given H-complement equals 0.08.
beats.whyThisBeat: Introduce the complement hypothesis and false-positive rate needed for the denominator

beats.text: The denominator of Bayes' rule is P of E, the total probability of getting a positive test regardless of disease status — called the marginal probability of E. We compute it by summing over both possibilities: P of E equals P of E given H times P of H, plus P of E given H-complement times P of H-complement.
beats.whyThisBeat: Define marginal probability P(E) via the law of total probability, rung 3

beats.text: Plugging in: 0.9 times 0.01 plus 0.08 times 0.99, which gives 0.009 plus 0.0792, equaling 0.0882.
beats.whyThisBeat: Compute P(E) numerically to make the denominator concrete

beats.text: Now Bayes' rule says P of H given E equals P of E given H times P of H, divided by P of E. That is 0.009 divided by 0.0882, which is approximately 0.102 — about ten percent.
beats.whyThisBeat: State and apply Bayes' rule formula, rung 4, delivering the numerical result

beats.text: Even after a positive test, the posterior is only ten percent because the disease is rare. The key insight is that the numerator is just likelihood times prior.
beats.whyThisBeat: Build intuition about base-rate dominance, rung 5 and rung 7

beats.text: So we say the posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior. The denominator P of E is simply a normalizing constant that makes probabilities sum to one. Write it with the proportionality symbol: P of H given E is proportional to P of E given H times P of H. That proportionality is the engine of Bayesian reasoning.
beats.whyThisBeat: Cement the proportionality shorthand and explain the normalizing constant role, rungs 5 and 6

actions.content: Likelihood: P(E|H) = P(positive test | patient sick)
Marginal probability: P(E) = total chance of positive test
Disease is rare -> base rate dominates -> posterior stays low
P(E) = normalizing constant (makes probabilities sum to 1)
draw bracket spanning P(E|H)*P(H) in eq_bayes_full labeled numerator=likelihood*prior, and arrow pointing down to eq_proportional labeled drop denominator to get proportionality
